message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="heydon-pickering" color="red" summary="Revised retro: agree on atomicity concern, a11y review timing">
Kent, good observations. I want to refine my earlier assessment in light of your more careful reading of the pipeline.

**(a) Pipeline compliance**: You are right to question this. The pipeline says "the smallest meaningful change" at step 1. I confirmed the axe-core tests pass and the a11y concerns are addressed, but I did not scrutinize whether the Driver followed strict red-green-refactor cycles within that single commit. From the outside, we cannot tell whether there were intermediate TDD cycles — the git history shows one large commit, not a trail of small steps. That is a process gap regardless of whether the Driver was disciplined internally.

**(b) Atomicity**: I agree with your decomposition. From an accessibility perspective specifically, the a11y-relevant pieces — semantic HTML in templates, aria-labels, focus management, axe-core tests — were bundled with everything else. If the templates had been a separate commit, I could have reviewed the markup, labels, and ARIA usage in isolation and given focused feedback before the Driver moved on to CSS or e2e tests. Instead, the review was post-hoc on a finished feature. That weakens the continuous review aspect of our process.

**(c) Process improvement**: I support your proposal for smaller commits per layer or slice. From my side, I would add: the accessibility review is most valuable when it happens at the template/markup stage, not after everything is wired up. If we commit the templates and route handlers as one atomic step, I can verify semantic structure, labels, and ARIA before the Driver moves to styling or e2e. That way, structural a11y issues get caught early rather than requiring rework of downstream layers.

I also still want to flag the focus-after-delete item for `docs/deferred-items.md` — that applies across inbox and contexts and should be tracked as a cross-cutting concern.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Heydon's in — agreed on all three action items, and makes the same feedback-loop argument from the accessibility angle. Eight of nine now. Just Marty left.
